title: STANDARD FOR QUICK FROZEN BLOCKS OF FISH FILLET, MINCED FISH FLESH AND MIXTURES OF FILLETS AND MINCED FISH FLESH

sections.section: STANDARD FOR QUICK FROZEN BLOCKS OF FISH FILLET, MINCED FISH FLESH AND MIXTURES OF FILLETS AND MINCED FISH FLESH
sections.summary: Document title only: Standard for Quick Frozen Blocks of Fish Fillet, Minced Fish Flesh and Mixtures of Fillets and Minced Fish Flesh.
Scope: applies to quick frozen blocks of cohering fish flesh made from fillets, minced fish flesh, or mixtures thereof that are intended for further processing.

Describes product, processing and quality requirements: quick frozen blocks may comprise single or mixed species with similar sensory traits, fillets and minced flesh are defined, freezing must rapidly pass the maximum crystallization range and reach −18°C or colder at the thermal centre after stabilization, industrial repacking/reprocessing is permitted under controlled conditions with reapplication of quick freezing, products must be packaged to minimize dehydration/oxidation, glazing water must be potable or clean sea‑water meeting potable microbiological standards, all ingredients must be food grade, histamine in specified families must not exceed 10 mg/100 g (average), and final product compliance is assessed by the methods and lot examination provisions in Sections 7–9.

Defines quick frozen blocks as uniformly shaped masses of cohering fillets, minced fish, or mixtures thereof (single species or mixtures with similar sensory characteristics), defines fillets as slices cut parallel to the backbone, and defines minced fish flesh as particles of skeletal muscle essentially free from bones, viscera and skin.

Specifies freezing/process conditions: product must be quick‑frozen by passing the maximum crystallization range rapidly and is not regarded as frozen until the thermal centre reaches −18°C or colder after stabilization, must be kept deep frozen during distribution, industrial repacking/further processing is permitted under controlled conditions with reapplication of quick freezing, and processing/packaging must minimize dehydration and oxidation.

Any presentation of quick frozen blocks is permitted provided it meets all requirements of the standard and is adequately described on the label to avoid misleading consumers; blocks may be presented as boneless only if boning (including removal of pin-bones) has been completed.

Quick frozen blocks must be made from fillets or minced flesh of sound fish fit for sale as fresh, glazing water (if used) must be potable or clean sea-water meeting WHO-equivalent microbiological standards, other ingredients must be food grade and conform to Codex standards, histamine for specified families must not exceed 10 mg/100 g (average of sample unit), and final product conformity is determined by examination methods and lot provisions referenced in Sections 7–9.
Quick frozen blocks shall be prepared from fillets or minced flesh of sound fish of a quality fit to be sold fresh for human consumption.

If glazed, water used for glazing or glazing solutions must be potable (meeting WHO International Guidelines for Drinking Water Quality) or clean sea-water that meets the same microbiological standards and is free from objectionable substances.
All other ingredients used in the product must be of food-grade quality and conform to applicable Codex standards.

Products shall not contain more than 10 mg/100 g of histamine (based on the average of the sample unit tested), and this limit applies only to species of the Clupeidae, Scombridae, Scombresocidae, Pomatomidae and Coryphaenedae families.
A product is considered to meet the standard when lots examined per Section 9 comply with provisions in Section 8, and products shall be examined using the methods given in Section 7.

Acidity regulators, antioxidants, humectants and thickeners are acceptable for use in foods conforming to this standard if used in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CODEX STAN 192‑1995) for food category 09.2.1 and its parent categories.

Products should be prepared and handled following relevant Codex hygiene and fishery practice codes and must comply with any Codex microbiological criteria; the final product must be free from foreign material that threatens health, and when tested it must not contain harmful microorganisms or substances in amounts that represent a health hazard, including histamine not exceeding 20 mg/100 g in any sample unit for specified fish families, nor other hazardous substances exceeding Codex limits.

Introductory heading stating that, in addition to the General Standard for the Labelling of Prepackaged Foods, the following specific labelling provisions apply to these products (substantive provisions follow in subsequent subsections).

The food name must be declared as "x y blocks" (x = common species name(s), y = form of presentation), state if glazed with sea-water, include the term "quick frozen" (or locally customary "frozen" where applicable), declare percentage ranges for mince when it exceeds 10% of net fish content (with >90% regarded as mince blocks), and state storage conditions to maintain quality during transport, storage and distribution.
For glazed products, the declared net contents must exclude the weight of the glaze.
Labels must indicate that the product shall be stored at a temperature of −18 °C or colder.

Required labelling information may be on the container or accompanying documents, except the product name, lot identification, name and address of manufacturer/packer and storage instructions must appear on the container, and lot identification or name/address may be replaced by an identification mark if it is clearly identifiable with accompanying documents.

This section prescribes the lot sampling plan (entire block as sample unit) with sample sizes and acceptance numbers, requires sensory and physical assessment by trained personnel per referenced procedures, and details analytical/examination methods including net weight determination for glazed and non‑glazed products, parasite detection (Type I), methods for determining fillet versus mince proportions and gelatinous condition, cooking and thawing procedures, and histamine determination performance criteria for MLs of 10 mg/100 g (average) and 20 mg/100 g (each unit).

Sampling of lots uses the entire block as the sample unit with specified sample sizes and acceptance numbers by lot size (table provided); accept the lot if defective blocks ≤ acceptance number c, otherwise reject, and net weight sampling follows an appropriate Codex sampling plan.

Samples for sensory and physical examination must be assessed by trained personnel following the procedures in Sections 7.3–7.7, Annex A, and the Guidelines for the Sensory Evaluation of Fish and Shellfish in Laboratories (CAC/GL 31‑1999).

Net weight determination procedures are specified for non‑glazed product (weigh in frozen state) and glazed product (remove visible/felt ice glaze under a gentle cold spray, blot off water and weigh), with an alternate method referenced in Annex B.

sections.section: 7.3.1 Determination of Net Weight of Product Not Covered by Glaze
sections.summary: The net weight (exclusive of packaging) of each sample unit not covered by glaze shall be determined in the frozen state.

sections.section: 7.3.2 Determination of Net Weight of Products Covered by Glaze
sections.summary: For glazed products, remove the package from frozen storage, rinse under a gentle spray of cold water to remove all visible/felt ice glaze, remove adhering water with paper towel and weigh the product, with an alternate method described in Annex B.

sections.section: 7.4 Procedure for the Detection of Parasites for skinless blocks of fish fillets (Type I method)
sections.summary: Describes a non‑destructive Type I inspection for parasites in skinless blocks of fish fillets: examine the entire thawed sample unit by placing portions on a 5 mm acrylic sheet (45% translucency) and candle with a 1500 lux light source positioned 30 cm above the sheet.

sections.section: 7.5 Determination  of  Proportions  of  Fillet  and  Minced  Fish  in  Quick  Frozen  Blocks  prepared  from Mixtures of Fillets and Minced Fish  2 3
sections.summary: Specifies that proportions of fillet and minced fish in quick‑frozen mixed blocks are determined using the AOAC “Physical Separation of Fillets and Minced Fish” method (AOAC 988.09).

Refers to AOAC moisture determination procedures (AOAC 1990, 983.18 and 950.46) for assessing gelatinous condition, noting the method has been evaluated for cod only and is accurate for mince levels above 10%.

Sets cooking procedures used to confirm sensory/texture defects: heat product to an internal temperature of 65–70°C (avoid overcooking) and describes recommended baking, steaming, boil‑in‑bag and microwave techniques with procedural notes.

sections.section: 7.8. Thawing Procedure for Quick Frozen Blocks
sections.summary: Provides two thawing methods for quick‑frozen blocks: Air thaw in sealed impermeable bags or humidity‑controlled environment (≥80% RH) at ≤25°C with internal block temperature not exceeding 7°C, and water immersion with sealed bags in circulating water at 21°C ±1.5°C, both ending when product separates without tearing.

Requires histamine analytical methods to meet specified performance criteria (tables): MLs of 10 mg/100 g (average) and 20 mg/100 g (per unit) with defined applicable ranges, LOD/LOQ, RSDR, recoveries (90–107%) and lists compliant methods (AOAC 977.13; NMKL 99; NMKL 196).

Defines a sample unit as defective if it exhibits any listed properties, namely deep dehydration, foreign matter, parasites, bones (in boneless packs), objectionable odour/flavour, or flesh abnormalities, with specific criteria for each defect type.

Defines deep dehydration as when >10% of the sample unit’s surface shows excessive moisture loss (white or yellow discoloration) penetrating below the surface and not removable by scraping without unduly affecting appearance.

Defines foreign matter as any non‑fish material in the sample unit (excluding packing) that does not pose a health threat but is readily recognizable without magnification or present at a level indicating non‑compliance with good manufacturing and sanitation practices.
Defines parasite defects as the presence of two or more parasites per kg detected by the method in Section 7.4 with a capsular diameter >3 mm or non‑encapsulated parasites >10 mm in length.

Specifies bone defects for packs labeled boneless: more than one bone per kg ≥10 mm length or ≥1 mm diameter is defective; bones ≤5 mm length are not defects if diameter ≤2 mm; vertebral ‘foot’ is disregarded if ≤2 mm wide or easily removed with a fingernail.
Declares a sample unit defective if affected by persistent, distinct objectionable odours or flavours indicative of decomposition, rancidity, or feed.

Defines flesh abnormalities as excessive gelatinous condition accompanied by >86% moisture in any individual fillet, or a pasty texture from parasitic infestation affecting >5% of the sample unit by weight.

Sets lot acceptance criteria: the lot meets the standard if (i) defective sample units do not exceed the acceptance number (c) of the sampling plan in Section 7, (ii) the average net weight of sample units is at least the declared weight with no unreasonable shortages, and (iii) Food Additives, Hygiene and Labelling requirements in Sections 4–6 are satisfied.

Outlines sensory and physical examination steps: determine net weight, measure dehydrated areas removable only with a knife to calculate percentage affected, thaw and inspect each block for foreign matter, bones, odour and texture, and resolve inconclusive odour or gelatinous assessments by cooking per Section 7.7 or moisture determination per Section 7.6.

Annex A describes a validated method for determining net content of glazed frozen fish blocks (mainly brown/fatty species destined for further processing), giving principle, equipment, sample preparation, deglazing procedure, and calculations to derive net and glaze weights.

Principle: remove glaze from a pre‑weighed frozen sample by immersing in a water bath until the surface becomes rough to the touch, then dry and reweigh repeatedly to estimate net product content while avoiding thaw drip or re‑freezing losses.

Lists required equipment for deglazing and net‑weight determination: balance (1 g sensitivity), adjustable water bath, 20 cm circular sieve (1–3 mm mesh), smooth towels and a freezer box at the work station.

sections.section: 3. PREPARATION OF SAMPLES AND WATER BATH
sections.summary: Sample and water bath preparation: adjust product to −18/−20°C, remove external ice/snow from packaging, and prepare a potable water bath volume ≈10× declared product weight at 15–35°C.
Gross‑weight determination (A): after removing packaging weigh the glazed product (record individual weights A1–An for single fillets) and place weighed samples into a freezer box pending deglazing.

Procedure for removal of external glaze from pre-weighed samples: immerse samples in a water bath and agitate by hand until glaze is no longer felt (typically 10–60 s, longer if heavily glazed or frozen together); for block-frozen consumer packs, transfer the block to a sieve, immerse and separate deglazed portions by slight finger pressure, repeating short immersions if residues remain.

Immediately weigh the deglazed sample/sub-sample after removing adhering water with a towel without applying pressure, and sum the individual net weights of sub-samples (B1–Bn) to obtain net weight B.
Defines glaze weight C as the difference between gross weight A and net weight B (C = A − B).

Provides formulas to calculate percentage glaze: percent glaze expressed relative to the gross product weight (G) and relative to the net product weight (H), with C as the glaze weight and multiplication by 100 to obtain percentages.
